title: Keynote Speech by Dr. Stacey Gabriel (Broad Institute)
text:
And it is my true pleasure to introduce
Stacy Gabriel. Stacy Gabriel is
executive vice president and head of
platforms and scientific execution at
the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard
right here. Prior to her current role,
she led platform development, execution,
and operation for the genomics platform,
beginning with the Broad Institute's
founding in 2003. She is also an
institute scientist and serves on the
institute's executive leadership team.
Stacy Gabriel is widely recognized as a
leader in genomics technology and
genomics application to foundational
research. Her early work was
foundational to the HAP map project. I
have to admit I had to look that up. Is
the international scientific pro project
to develop a hletype map of the human
genome. She has led the Broad
Institute's contributions to numerous
flagship programs in human genetics
including the 10,00 genomes project, the
cancer genome atlas and the topmet
program. She's also principal
investigator of the Broad Institute's
Oliver's Genomics Center and serves on
its program steering committee. Stacy,
it's a true pleasure to have you here
today. Thank you very much for joining
us.
>> Great. Hello.
Wonderful.
Well, it's really nice to be here. Um
this is such a great topic and I I will
say that our our director, Dr. Todd
Gallib, does send his regrets. he was
called to um Washington DC to um an NIH
meeting related to our future sort of
budgetary outlook for federal funding.
So you can imagine why that's an
important subject. So Todd does send his
regrets but and he he would have liked
to have been here as well. Um so I I
thought about how I might approach this
sort of brief keynote with you all and
and um I wanted to start here and say
that the Broad Institute just celebrated
its 20th birthday. Um I have been here
for 26 years. So I was here well before
the broad actually got it started. I I
started next door at the Whitehead
Genome Center. But um so we've been
around for 20 years and um we had our
20th ann we had our 20th scientific
retreat recently. Um and at that retreat
a few months ago um we took some time to
reflect on what you know I would
consider these what we consider these
revolutions in science that have
occurred. Many of them starting here at
the bro have been all of them involving
you know massive sort of national and
international collaboration but there's
been some major revolutions um in biio
medicine that have occurred over the
past 20 years but rather than stop and
really dwell too much on these uh we
were asked a few of us were asked well
what are the next so what do you see as
the next revolutions that we might be
talking about at our 30th or 40th um
broad retreat um and so I thought well
since we're talking about the future in
biomedical research here well why don't
I share with you um three of those sort
of future revolutions that I think are
particularly important um and talk a bit
about how we might get there and why
they're impactful. Um, so I'm going to
get to my three things, but wanted to
start um before and just just talk a
little bit about how we achieve
revolutions and sort of what that
approach is um and reflect on what I've
seen um as sort of critical to some
success over here um here over the past
20 years having seen a lot of those
revolutions been involved in lot a lot
of those sort of revolutions in the
past. So first, you know, our approach
ultimately is to empower the next
generation of biomedical scientists to
come together and tackle large
collaborative projects that typically
can't be readily done in a traditional
research setting. That's always been the
idea behind the broad. Um, and then
there's a couple things though to bring
together with that approach. And the
first is vision. So what do you want to
accomplish and why? What do you intend
to change? Um, often those come with the
second thing. So flagship efforts that
create communities, push boundaries and
drive us to scale or create some sort of
capability or access that wasn't
possible in the past. Um these can like
I said they can be capabilities, they
can be methods, they can be data sets.
Oftentimes they are sort of foundational
data sets that make their way to the
rest of the world um that we can work on
together with with colleagues from all
over the place. But typically it's some
of those early flagship efforts that
really drive those things forward. And
some of those were mentioned in this in
the kind introduction. Things like the
cancer genome atlas. We started working
on the cancer genome atlas. We couldn't
perform exom sequencing. It seemed like
an impossibility at the time. But
because we had this very important
imperative to be able to catalog tumor
specific DNA changes across the whole
genome that really pushed us to develop
and really optimize exom sequencing to
what it is today just as an example. And
finally a big part is the model sort of
the way we do approach things here um at
the broad the people and the um and
bringing together that ability to and
focus on execution and just to give a
sense of that. So when we think about
the biomedical sort of ecosystem,
there's typically like I would say two
two sort of buckets you find yourself
in, right? Either traditional academia
where there's trainees that are being
led by a faculty member pursuing, you
know, exploratory basic science or
clinical research. On the other hand,
you typically have industry. So industry
sort of brings together these really
goal focused teams that are developing
products very focused on the potential
for financial return.
I think what we've really been trying to
do here over the past couple decades is
sort of bring together sort of a nice
compromise um and meet in the middle and
say how can we take the best of both
worlds and meld sort of that academic
creativity with industrial scale and
sort of a focus on sort of that goal
oriented sort of pro pro project driven
driven science um but we don't focus on
financial return although the finances
are important um so why Todd's at the
NIH today. Um, but really the focus here
is on on on science for the public good
and we are seeking with our science to
accelerate progress for the entirety of
the academic research well the whole
research ecosystem.
Um, and before again before I get to the
um to the to the sort of revolution
ideas just one more thing that I I feel
is is important to frame. So some of the
things whenever we all came up with our
sort of next revolutions things seem
like things that are impossible. Oh, I
can never imagine how we're going to get
there. And just to step back and and and
just sort of remind that like when we
started the Broad Institute, some of the
things we're doing today now quite
routinely seemed impossible at the time
too, even a few, you know, a decade ago.
So if you think about 2004 when we all
got started here, we basically had one
genome available to us. It was the
genome sequence as part of the human
genome project a huge international
consortium. Now we routinely are
sequence sequencing hundreds of
thousands of human genomes for research
projects. UK bio bank 500,000 genomes.
The all of us research project which is
our US version of a biioank 500,000
genome. So this is quite routine. Now if
you think about the field of single cell
sequencing so the ability to take one
cell and capture the whole RNA
transcriptum for that one cell. Well,
the very first paper around 2013 or 14
was a paper that described the
sequencing of about 18 cells. Like that
got a nature paper from researchers here
and collaborators across the world. Now
we are routinely performing experiments
of hundreds of thousands of cells and
reading out that transcryto and we're
well on our way to being able to
routinely perform million cell
experiments and then billion cell
experiments. And I'll talk a little bit
about that. But those are the kind of
things and just to frame that these
things change quickly and they change
because we take time to grow and learn
how to automate lower costs and be
systematic and then we sort of then push
those into sort of a production phase.
So I'll start with that first sort of
revolutionary idea or where I think we
should be um if we come together in you
know the next sort of 10 or 20 years and
the first idea is genomes for everybody
not just in the research setting. So
what I mean by this is we'd like to be
able to provide complete interpreted
genome sequence results from sample to
answer. So sample comes in, report comes
out to be used in routine clinical care
as part of the medical record. Some
things are already happening now. This
is not like this is an entirely new idea
or untapped potential entirely. We know
that in newborn screening there are
pilot programs now where thousands of
babies have been sequenced have have
been used whole genome sequencing um in
addition to sort of standard newborn
screening to understand what you might
get sort of are you going to miss some
things how much more do you pick up and
this is already happening and there's
already evidence that um it does impact
patient care and you can find things
with the genome you would have missed by
that traditional newborn screening. Um
we know that there's programs where to
replace traditional assays like
chromosomal microarrays for the
detection of neurodedevelopmental
disease with standard whole genome
sequencing that gives you more
information for essentially the same
cost in the diagnostic setting. And
finally polygenic risk scores are an
interesting sort of area where a simple
whole genome sequence can give you your
risk score already for tens of common
conditions. And this kind of information
can be integrated um as part of a
clinical workup. you know, in addition
to things like your family history,
your, you know, your statin level, your
cholesterol level, why not include your
polygenic risk score for that trait as
well. So, this is already happening. But
to get to that next stage where we're
saying this is going to be routine and
part of the medical record, a lot is
going to need to happen and a lot is
going to need to change. First of all,
the operational implementation of of
this, I think particularly in the US,
seems quite impossible to me. Sometimes
when I think about how fragmented things
are about the with our with our e EHR
systems with the way tests get ordered
for from doctors to labs. Um having
lived through developing a COVID testing
operation I can tell you that was
complicated enough for a PCR test. I
can't imagine getting a whole genome
sequence um reported out to this sort of
fragmented healthcare we have. So that's
I think somewhere where we will look to
sort of you know international partners
that have I think you know simpler and
more straightforward frameworks for EHRs
that um we've already seen success in
some of the European countries that I
think we're quite envious of in the UK
for example. Um so that's that's a big
piece of it. And of course there's the
regulatory framework. Something's going
to have to change there to really you
know force and sort of understand with
insurers how we're going to actually get
this done in a in a way that is is is
acceptable to all. Of course, we need to
continue to demonstrate utility. Why do
you want to have the whole genome
sequence and then do enormous studies of
the ultimate health care sort of
economic impact of having this kind of
sequencing added to the healthcare um
record. But I think ultimately this is
what will be needed to to move to this
next stage.
So again, because we're moving um
rapidly through what these revolutions
might be, I'm going to give you a second
one and that is the idea of multiomics
at scale. So, not just genomics, but the
ability to
deploy deep molecular profiling in a
longitudinal bioank setting um and even
potentially in a learning healthcare
setting because that ultimately would be
the best bioank setting because you're
keeping track of someone over time to
drive a deeper understanding of disease
classification outcomes and risk
prediction. So already now lots of
multiomic things are happening. So ohms
beyond the genome. So we can study the
plasma proteome. So 5,000 proteins in a
plasma sample for a couple hundred
dollars in a sample right now. Um
discovery proteomics goes deeper and can
will study proteins in an unsort of
targeted manner. We could perform this
now for thousands of proteins and
thousands of samples simultaneously.
Metabolomics. So understanding the
metabolites already being done at the
bioank scale. um the UK bio bank is
embarking upon a large metabolomic study
and then there's other things like the
epiggenome or the single cell
transcriptto um that are expanding
but to get to that sort of next level
well I really think that is where the
idea of that longitudinal bioank setting
becomes so important because ultimately
you do want to understand how these
profiles change over time as people age
as they're exposed to different sort of
environmental sort of toxins or if they
stay healthy, if they're not exposed to
anything. So, how will we ever make
those connections if we don't have this
kind of bioank setting? Ultimately,
these readouts are going to need to
become cheaper and more scalable so we
can perform them at a scale where you
can actually find the end point. If
these studies always say very small and
targeted, well, it takes a long time to
collect enough sort of people who have
had a myioardial inffection to say, I
can understand how that's changed. So
really, that's really where the
technology will need to be that we're
able to perform very simple and
straightforward readouts so we can
really do this at the scale of hundreds
of thousands if not millions of
individuals. And of course, new many new
analytic methods will be needed to
really be able to integrate and
harmonize this type of data. But that is
the goal ahead.
Oh, I'm going backwards. That's not
right. And then the third the third sort
of revolution idea and the one that I
think I'm probably most excited about
from a technology point of view is the
idea of cellular perturbations and
readouts at high scale. So the idea here
and the vision is that we can generate
and analyze perturbational data set. So
genomewide partibation across many cell
types coupled to um either genomic or
imaging readouts that can turn that list
of genetic variants that are associated
with disease into that functional
insight. And it's that functional
insight we need to understand that might
eventually lead us to understand how we
might target that that gene to impact
disease. And so how how will this
change? So right now and what's so
exciting is that we know we can use gene
editing tools to systematically install
any sequence change in any cell and then
determine the consequence of that
sequence change of that perturbation by
a molecular or an imaging readout. So
this is already happening in sort of
various pockets all over the world
certainly happening a lot here at the
broad. Um, but what we're really talking
about now is performing this in a sort
of a much more systematic high-scale way
so that we can really turn this into
genomewide analyses across many many
different cell types. Um, and what's
enabling this is is the massively sort
of, you know, is partnering with
technology um, companies, partnering
with with collaborators all over the
place to really drive down some of these
costs. And to give you a sense, if if
you would if you were to ask me what a
single cell transcriptal transcriptto
profile would have cost a couple years
ago, it was like something like 2550
cents per cell to get a whole
transcriptto for a single cell. Now
we're at the point where it's a couple
pennies and I can see a very clear path
to that being, you know, less than a
penny within a year or two's time. And
this is because the cost of sequencing
is getting cheaper. The cost of the prep
is getting cheaper. And we're able to
perform these experiments at very high
scale. We're actually building a
platform for this now that will is
really going to be done in four parts.
The four parts of that platform for for
cell um perturbation will include
various types of inputs whether they're
single cells. They come to us as single
cells. Whether it's a tumor sample or a
tissue sample or um an inducible stem
cell that could come to us. a next step
of perturbing that cell in different
ways whether it's by crisper of knock in
or knock out or activating or perturbing
that cell with with with compounds with
drug treatments um then performing a
variety of readouts next is the is the
next stage and then there's there's
downstream data analysis ultimately this
this kind of activity is going to
produce massive data sets that are going
to really be the fuel for those sort of
those AI enabled tools that are going to
be able to turn that all that data you
know we hope into into great insights
and the the reason this is I would say
different than the way we've thought
about it you know in the past is that
you know we've known about many many
genes and many many variants that that
are associated with disease for a long
time and so far it's I would say an
exercise of kind of like hand-tohand
combat to go after that gene or that
variant and that disease sort of one at
a time the idea here is we want to
perform this in a very sort of
systematic routine way to bring all this
data together in a way that can power um
these new analytic approaches. Um I
don't have time to talk to you about
some of the I I would say the
operational approaches that will underly
um our being able to perform this in
this very scalable way, but I will point
you to a book that just um got published
um by collaborators of ours at MIT um
Sloan School of Management. So we work
with a few faculty members there who
have really been instrumental in helping
us design our systemat or I would call
them our industrial work design process.
There's really led to I think a lot of
how we've scaled our activities. Um they
do tell the wonderful story I think of
how we built the COVID testing operation
here at the bro. It's weaved through
this book quite a bit. Um, so I just
point that out as a way to sort of lo
learn more about our approach because I
don't have time to go in today. And
finally, because I was asked to comment
on on examples of sort of international
collaborations and the importance of
international collaboration to us, which
they certainly are, I just point to this
example of of this really nice um
relationship that has sprung up between
Denmark and and um Kendall Square and
the Broad Institute here. And this was
really um this was really led by the
Novo Nordisk Foundation who um came to
us and they said, "Hey, you know,
obviously big interest in metabolic
disease in Denmark and and in within
Novo. A lot of, you know, great science
here um around metabolic disease." And
in fact, a lot of that work I just
talked to you about related to the cell
factory is a big pillar of this
relationship um with the Noto Novo
Foundation. Um and so this has been a
really neat partnership that brings
together scientists from Denmark. They
come here and spend time. Our scientists
go there and spend time and um really
working around common collaborative
projects. And so it's been a really nice
relationship that I think has been going
on now for four or five years and is set
to be renewed for another another term.
And so I'm going to um wrap up and thank
you for your attention in my whirlwind
of of three possible future revolutions
in in the biomedical uh research. some
of the things that go on here. Um, you
know, I think that the goal is really
that we sharpen and scale these genomic
tools to increase the breadth and the
depth of our of the idea of the genotype
to phenotype correlation and really
getting to that next level. Um, we will
require systematic and well-ontrolled
data generation to provide that
foundational data that will be shared
broadly and that will um really be the
underlying that future application of AI
and creating models. Um this will
require really tight partnerships
between biologists, disease expert,
technologists, operations and
computational experts. And um we
certainly can't do it alone. Um and
we'll depend on many sort of
communitywide partnerships,
international partnerships um and to
really build on those kind of those past
successes that we've been I think so
fortunate to be a part of. So that's it
and thank you very much for your
attention.
